retrieved_docs.title: Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: LEMS is a B-cell–mediated autoimmune neurological disorder specifically targeting the cell surface antigen (VGCC). The VGCC-IgG antibody is central to this disorder and directly contributes to its pathogenesis, leading to compromised neuromuscular transmission and manifesting as observable muscle weakness. Profoundly diminished deep tendon reflexes serve as a distinctive hallmark.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Junction -- Clinical Significance -- Myasthenia Gravis
retrieved_docs.content: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disorder characterized by autoantibodies targeting AChRs or MuSK at the NMJ. Clinically, this condition presents as fluctuating muscle weakness that worsens with activity and improves with rest. The initial symptoms typically include extraocular muscle dysfunction, causing ptosis and diplopia. The condition may later affect the bulbar muscles, leading to dysphagia, and the limb muscles, causing generalized weakness. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Neurologic Disorder -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Myasthenia gravis – Myasthenia gravis is a disorder of neuromuscular transmission characterized by weakness in the bulbar, ocular, respiratory, and extremity muscles. Aside from history and physical exam, serologic testing will often reveal positive antibodies against the acetylcholine receptor (AChR-Ab) and/or positive antibodies against muscle-specific tyrosine kinase (MuSK-Ab). Electrodiagnostic tests, such as repetitive nerve stimulation and single-fiber electromyography, can also help diagnose myasthenia gravis. Stroke – Strokes will have characteristics and anatomical patterns of the motor, sensory, or generalized neurologic changes. Spinal disorders – There are many disorders of the spinal cord that cause motor and sensory disturbances, which patients may mimic in functional neurologic disorder. Some of these conditions include cervical myelopathy, spinal stenosis, etc.

retrieved_docs.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple sclerosis can present with any neurological sign that may fluctuate or persist over hours to days to weeks secondary to demyelination in the central nervous system. This may present with weakness, sensory deficits, cognitive and behavioral issues. Weakness may be unilateral or bilateral and is usually accompanied by upper motor neuron signs like hyperreflexia, spasticity, and a positive Babinski.

retrieved_docs.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Class II: Involves mild weakness of muscles other than ocular muscles. Ocular muscle weakness of any severity may be present.

retrieved_docs.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: MuSK MG has clinical features that are quite different from n-AChR MG . It is more common in females, relatively spares extraocular muscles, and commonly involves bulbar, facial, and neck muscles. Myasthenic crisis is also frequent in the MuSK MG . [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Extraocular Muscle Weakness: Around 85% of patients will have this on the initial presentation. Common patient complaints include diplopia, ptosis, or both. These symptoms can progress and cause generalized MG involving the bulbar, axial, and limb muscles in 50% of patients in two years. [10] Bulbar Muscle Weakness: This can be the initial presentation in 15% of patients and causes symptoms like difficulty chewing or frequent choking, dysphagia, hoarseness, and dysarthria. [10] The involvement of facial muscles causes an expressionless face, and neck muscle involvement causes a dropped-head syndrome. Limb Weakness: This usually involves the proximal muscles more than distal muscles, with the upper limbs more affected than the lower limbs. Myasthenic crisis: It is due to the involvement of intercostal muscles and diaphragm and is a medical emergency.

retrieved_docs.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Class I: Involves any ocular muscle weakness, including weakness of eye closure. All other muscle groups are normal.

retrieved_docs.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Class IV: Involves severe weakness of affected muscles. Ocular muscle weakness of any severity can be present.

retrieved_docs.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The physical examination may reveal normal muscle power because of the fluctuating disease pattern. In such cases, repeated or sustained muscle contractions can demonstrate weakness. Improvement is seen after a period of rest or application of ice (ice-pack test) to the muscle group involved. The pupils, deep tendon reflexes, and sensory examinations are normal.

retrieved_docs.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Class IVa: Involves limb, axial muscles, or both predominantly. Oropharyngeal muscles can be involved to a lesser degree.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Transmission -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: The detection of specific antibodies in a patient's serum aids in diagnosing myasthenia gravis and LES, both of which are autoimmune diseases. Approximately 85% of patients with generalized myasthenia gravis and 50% of those with only ocular myasthenia gravis have ACh receptor antibodies in their serum. For patients with ocular myasthenia gravis who lack ACh receptor antibodies in their serum, detecting muscle-specific kinase (MuSK) antibodies can be helpful for diagnosis. A positive serum voltage-gated calcium channel antibody test, along with characteristic EMG findings, can assist in diagnosing LES. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Diplopia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: If fatiguability, variability, decreased orbicularis strength, or any other symptoms of ocular or generalized myasthenia gravis are present, acetylcholine receptor antibody testing should be performed, although its sensitivity is only about 50% to 70%. [22] If the diagnosis is strongly suspected despite the negative acetylcholine receptor antibody titers, a single fiber electromyogram of the orbicularis muscle should be performed, as in the hands of an experienced operator, it has a sensitivity of about 95% in diagnosing abnormalities specific to ocular myasthenia. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Class III: Involves muscles other than ocular muscles moderately. Ocular muscle weakness of any severity can be present.

retrieved_docs.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Class IIa: Involves predominant weakness of the limb, axial muscles, or both. It may also involve the oropharyngeal muscles to a lesser extent.

retrieved_docs.title: Ptosis Correction -- Introduction -- Myasthenia gravis
retrieved_docs.content: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disorder characterized by the presence of antibodies to acetylcholine receptors located at the neuromuscular endplates of voluntary muscles. [3] This leads to decreased action of acetylcholine which results in muscle weakness and fatigue. Myasthenia may be generalized or localized to the eye (ocular myasthenia). The most common presenting feature is variable ptosis associated with diplopia. Symptoms may be unilateral or bilateral. Patients with myasthenia initially have a good levator function. Prolonged upgaze will cause a worsening of ptosis in these patients due to muscle fatigue. Cogan lid twitch sign: Rapid saccadic eye movements from downgaze to primary position results in rapid upshoot of the lid followed by a gradual drop to the primary position. Other tests which help confirm the diagnosis include ice test, serum acetylcholine receptor antibody assay, single fiber electromyography, and repetitive nerve stimulation test.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2447
retrieved_docs.content: Hemiplegia and hemiparesis are clinical conditions that fall under the broader category of paralysis; they involve loss of motor function on one side of the body, with hemiplegia denoting complete weakness and hemiparesis indicating partial weakness. Recognizing these disorders as subtypes of paralysis helps clinicians diagnose the extent of neurological impairment and tailor rehabilitation and treatment strategies accordingly.

Overall, the interconnected knowledge emphasizes that استسقاء الدماغ is a central nervous system disorder with defined anatomical locations, characteristic clinical associations (especially with dementia), specific neuroimaging modalities (CT head and abnormal brain echo), and a set of neurosurgical interventions essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and comprehensive patient care.

This hierarchical relationship suggests that migraine and headache disorders may be viewed within the context of neuromuscular pathology, highlighting the importance of considering underlying neuromuscular factors when diagnosing and managing patients presenting with these headache syndromes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 1232
retrieved_docs.content: Non‑infectious encephalitis (SMQ) encompasses a range of neurobehavioral and motor disturbances. It includes اضطراب التصرف (behavior disorder) as a component, as well as both الاضطراب العابر في بدء الاستيقاظ أو المحافظة عليه (transient disorder in awakening onset or maintenance) and الاضطراب المستمر في بدء الاستيقاظ أو استمراره (persistent disorder in awakening onset or continuation). Additionally, فرط النشاط الحركي (hyperkinetic activity) and فرط النشاط العضلي (muscle hyperactivity) are clinical manifestations of SMQ. Together, these interconnected features characterize the complex presentation of التهاب الدماغ غير المعدي (SMQ) and are essential for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2499
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other disorders of nervous system” encompasses a diverse group of peripheral nerve and neuromuscular conditions. It is used to classify the nonspecific term “تكزز,” drug‑induced polyneuropathy (Polyneuropathie ten gevolge van geneesmiddelengebruik), idiopathic progressive neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب), multifocal neuropathy associated with malignant disease (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث), multifocal neuropathy linked to collagen‑vascular disease (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني), chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين), lower‑limb reflex sympathetic atrophy (الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي), and myasthenic syndrome (وهن عضلي وبيل). By grouping these varied entities under “Other disorders of nervous system,” clinicians are reminded that many neuropathic and neuromuscular disorders fall outside the more common diagnostic categories, prompting comprehensive evaluation and tailored management for patients presenting with unexplained nerve or muscle dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 260
retrieved_docs.content: observed in non‑infectious encephalitis (التهاب الدماغ غير المعدي) and non‑infectious delirium/brain dysfunction (الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي), where they co‑occur with motor and muscular hyperactivity and the transient and continuous awakening disorders that affect initiation and maintenance of vigilance. These overlapping symptom patterns underscore the importance of comprehensive assessment across depressive, encephalitic, and delirious presentations to inform accurate diagnosis and individualized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2390
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of nervous system is a diagnostic category that comprises a diverse group of neuropathic and inflammatory conditions. Within this category are the entities تكزز, اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب (idiopathic progressive neuropathy), اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد الكحولي (multifocal alcoholic neuropathy), اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة (severe neuropathy), اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين (chronic multifocal inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy), التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد (multifocal mononeuritis), الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي (reflexive atrophic changes of the lower limb), and وهن عضلي وبيل (muscular and peripheral weakness). Together, these conditions illustrate the breadth of disorders classified under other nervous system diseases, highlighting variations in etiology, severity, and anatomical distribution that are important for accurate diagnosis and targeted patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2380
retrieved_docs.content: Paralytisch syndroom, niet gespecificeerd is an unspecified paralytic syndrome that is classified as a subtype of paralysis. As a form of paralysis, it denotes a loss of muscle function without a more specific diagnostic label, and therefore shares the general clinical features, evaluation strategies, and management principles applied to paralysis as a whole. Recognizing this relationship helps clinicians approach the condition by assessing neurological deficits, investigating potential underlying causes, and implementing appropriate therapeutic interventions consistent with the broader category of paralysis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 90
retrieved_docs.content: pain (ألم), phantom‑limb syndrome (متلازمة الطرف الشبحي), sensory deficits, motor weakness, and paralysis (شلل). This framework supports precise anatomical localization, differential diagnosis, and therapeutic planning, especially the central role of nerve decompression procedures across multiple anatomical sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1416
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of nervous system constitute a broad diagnostic category that is classified as تكزز, اعتلال الأعصاب السكري, اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني, اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة, اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين, التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد, الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي, and وهن عضلي وبيل. These classifications encompass a range of neuropathic conditions, including diabetic neuropathy, multifocal neuropathy associated with collagen vascular disease, severe neuropathy, chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy, mononeuritis multiplex, reflexive sclerosis of the lower limb, and muscular and peripheral weakness. Recognizing that these diverse entities fall under the umbrella of other disorders of the nervous system aids clinicians in organizing differential diagnoses, guiding appropriate investigations, and tailoring management strategies for patients presenting with complex neurological symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 850
retrieved_docs.content: bronchitis (التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي) when it involves nerve findings, and muscular atrophy/Bell’s palsy (وهن عضلي وبيل), which reflect the nerves’ integral role in motor and sensory function. Together, these relationships delineate how a diverse spectrum of nervous‑system disorders is organized by their pathogenic mechanisms and anatomical involvement, providing a framework essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and comprehensive patient care.
